title: Machine Child

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Machine Child as a non-combat girl-raising/nurturing simulation. The gameplay loop centers on guiding a daughter through life in a town via 700+ story events, choosing activities, outfits, and developmental paths, building affection, and reaching 22 different endings. That strongly fits life_sim with branching, relationship-driven progression and replayable single-player outcomes. Visual-novel adjacency is present through authored event scenes and multiple endings, but the core identity is life simulation rather than pure VN.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Classic daughter-raising sim built around scheduling, stat growth, relationship shaping, outfits/events, and many possible adult outcomes.
Modern child-raising life sim where you guide a daughter through studies, activities, bonds, and heavily branching endings.
Choice- and stat-driven raising sim with weekly planning, event outcomes, and multiple endings based on how you develop the protagonist.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Ciel Fledge: A Daughter Raising Simulator
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Ciel Fledge: A Daughter Raising Simulator
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Explicit daughter-raising sim centered on training, schedule management, bonding, and branching story resolution.

matched_live_titles: Ciel Fledge: A Daughter Raising Simulator

current_live_neighbors.title: Ciel Fledge: A Daughter Raising Simulator

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Sigma Star Saga DX as a deliberate hybrid: top-down planetary RPG-style exploration combined with side-scrolling shoot-'em-up space battles, with branching story, multiple endings, boss battles, tool-based exploration, and weapon customization via Gun Data. While the evidence is strong for the game's mixed identity, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not cleanly cover this exploration-plus-shmup structure. Candidate RPG fits in the computed taxonomy are incomplete and rely on weak or mismatched assumptions about combat structure and perspective. Because its core gameplay loop materially spans an unsupported genre combination rather than aligning strongly to a single allowed archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate classification.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: A clear hybrid comp: campaign progression combines exploration, upgrades, and shmup-style bullet combat rather than treating shooting as a minor minigame. Structure differs as a metroidvania with continuous aerial combat, but it meaningfully overlaps the anchor's mixed shooter/adventure identity.

Strong match on top-down 2D action-RPG feel, tool-based exploration, puzzle gating, quests, and boss progression. It lacks the anchor's separate shmup battle layer, so it fits more as a structural neighbor than a truly close equivalent.
Exploration-led action game built around swapping between overworld traversal and distinct combat modes with ability-gated progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a restaurant-management/cooking simulation loop: inherit and restore a family restaurant, take customer orders, prepare meals through tactile cooking actions, upgrade tools and ingredients, customize the shop, and earn gameplay benefits from decorations. Progression is driven by improving the restaurant and building friendship levels with town residents, with a light authored story around rivals and family legacy. Although 'Meatball Brawls' are framed competitively, they are still culinary scoring duels rather than combat, so the core identity remains management sim/tycoon rather than action. Evidence is strong enough to accept management_tycoon, with some life-sim adjacency from relationship-building and town social elements.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close restaurant-sim match: fulfill customer orders, prepare food to spec, earn money, upgrade toppings/equipment/decor, and compete with a rival. Simpler scope than the anchor but highly aligned core loop.

Excellent restaurant-management comp with active cooking, menu building, kitchen customization, supplier/staff management, and business growth. More sim-heavy and less cozy/social than the anchor, but the gameplay identity is very close.

Cozy food-service management with hands-on baking, shop restoration, decoration, upgrades, and serving customers. The farm-to-table ingredient loop adds some divergence, but it matches the anchor's cozy management and customization well.

Good fit on restaurant revival, ingredient gathering, recipe learning, customer preference matching, and day/night service structure. Strong overlap with the anchor's social side, but the gathering/adventure rhythm makes it a bit less direct as a pure restaurant-management comp.
Cozy shop sim with direct customer service, venue upgrades, staff/menu management, and strong small-town relationship progression.

current_live_neighbors.title: The Lost Legends of Redwall: Feasts & Friends
The Lord of the Rings: Return to Moria

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently identify People of Note as a turn-based RPG centered on recruiting a band of party members, traveling across multiple themed regions, and progressing through a story campaign. The combat loop is explicitly party-based and tactical, with timing-based rhythm inputs layered onto turn-based encounters via musical mashup attacks and changing combat conditions. This supports a JRPG-story-RPG identity first, with tactical RPG elements secondary. The rhythm component appears additive to the core party-managed campaign RPG structure rather than defining the game as a standalone rhythm genre.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong fit: authored party-based JRPG with turn-based battles, exploration, and timed button presses that make combat actively interactive in a way close to the anchor.
Another close match on story-driven party RPG structure and action-command turn-based combat, though the available text gives less explicit combat detail than Super Mario RPG.
Strong modern comp for turn-based party RPG combat blended with real-time inputs, build synergies, and authored campaign progression, even if its tone and challenge profile are more intense.

Good broader JRPG match for party management, progression, dungeon runs, and strong authored presentation, but its social/calendar loop makes it structurally less direct than the active-input battle comps.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Mario & Luigi: Superstar Saga + Bowser's Minions
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Turn-based RPG where timed button inputs and reactive defense are central to every battle, with lighthearted story-driven party progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify the game as a cozy hidden-object adventure built around exploring hand-drawn city scenes, finding hidden objects, completing relaxing missions, unlocking new scenes, and decorating locations. The gameplay loop is discovery/puzzle-oriented rather than combat- or platform-focused, with single-player progression across handcrafted destinations. Metacritic also explicitly labels the genre as Hidden Object, reinforcing hidden_object_puzzle as the best fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Same developer and very close hidden-object loop: search detailed diorama-style scenes, complete relaxed objectives, and engage with light decoration/customization.
Relaxed hidden-object play across illustrated scenes with level progression and a strong user-created level sharing component.
Builds on the same cozy hidden-scene formula with more elaborate handcrafted environments and level creation/sharing features.
One of the clearest genre comps for scanning busy hand-drawn scenes to find specific objects and characters at a calm pace.
A similarly cozy seek-and-find experience in illustrated scenes, emphasizing relaxed exploration and object discovery over challenge or action.

title: A Rat's Quest: The Way Back Home - Season 1

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic copy consistently describe a single-player, narrative-driven action adventure centered on movement and navigation: 'climb, jump, crawl, slither, and sneak,' with explicit 'hybrid gameplay' switching between side and top-down perspectives. Combat is clearly present but not dominant over traversal, with a sword, throwing daggers, survival against threats, and 'challenging boss encounters.' The game is framed as a cinematic authored journey with voiced cutscenes, supporting a long-form linear campaign. While the cinematic framing is strong, the core playable identity in the evidence is platform-navigation-led action with stealth elements, making action_platformer the best fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Tiny rodent-led single-player action-adventure with strong stealth, agile navigation, dangerous oversized spaces, and a story quest; less platformer-structured and more immersive sim/RPG, but very close in audience and play feel.

Very close on cinematic traversal through hostile oversized environments, squeezing/climbing/hiding, and linear tension-driven progression; weaker on direct combat and lacks the rodent/adventure-romance identity.

Shares storybook tiny-hero action-adventure, traversal, combat, and environmental navigation, but VR framing and player-assist puzzle structure make its core loop meaningfully different from the anchor's standard action-platformer campaign.

Matches side-scrolling small-rat action, dangerous creatures, and boss-centric progression, but it is much more combat-heavy, RPG-geared, and punishing than the anchor's traversal-stealth-cinematic balance.
Cinematic side-scrolling stealth-platforming with a fragile hero sneaking through lethal oversized spaces and surviving high-pressure set pieces.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify All Will Fall as a 'survival city builder/colony sim' centered on physics-based 3D construction, resource management, logistics, production chains, societal needs, research progression, random events, and sandbox/scenario play. The core loop is building and optimizing a settlement rather than commanding armies or pursuing direct combat. Overhead strategy presentation, base/colony growth, systemic simulation, and optimization pressure strongly support city_builder as the primary archetype, with colony_sim and factory_builder as secondary overlaps due faction needs, survival management, and supply-chain emphasis.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Scenario-driven survival city building with harsh resource tradeoffs, population needs, political decisions, and constant pressure management.
Systems-heavy colony/city builder with vertical construction, infrastructure planning, production chains, and environmental survival constraints.
Post-apocalyptic survival colony management with research, resource chains, expeditions, and balancing settlement growth against recurring crises.
Pressure-heavy colony/city management built around layered infrastructure, workforce needs, exploration, and keeping a fragile habitat operational.

title: SHINOBI: Art of Vengeance - SEGA Villains DLC

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted via DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC is clearly additive rather than mode-shifting: it adds 5 stages, 3 bosses, ninpos, skins, music, and boss rush modes. The parent game's stored description explicitly defines the core gameplay as 'an all-new 2D action platformer' with precision ninja combat, combos, obstacle-overcoming tools, challenging platforming puzzles, hidden secrets, and multiple stages. That strongly supports action_platformer with side-scrolling 2D stage-based progression, dominant combat, platform traversal, boss encounters, and precision platforming. The DLC's boss-rush/stage content reinforces rather than contradicts that identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct parent game of the DLC and the clearest gameplay match: same 2D ninja action-platforming, combat toolkit, stage design, bosses, and progression systems.
Very close fit as a 2D ninja action-platformer with combat-heavy stages, precise movement, boss encounters, and unlockable abilities that reinforce the same audience expectation.
Another fast 2D ninja action-platformer with tight controls, enemy-filled traversal, upgrades, bosses, and branching paths, though it broadens into a more expansive adventure structure.

Shares side-scrolling combat, acrobatic movement, and sword-focused action, but its massive interconnected metroidvania world is a looser structural fit than the anchor’s stage-based action-platformer design.
Fast, demanding ninja combat with boss-heavy stage progression and precision action focus.

title: Cursed Words: The Word Game That Isn't

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cursed Words as a roguelike word/strategy game built around repeated runs, stage-based progression, synergy-building upgrades, and rule-bending modifiers. The Steam detailed description gives concrete loop evidence: each run has five stages with encounters and a boss, victory at Stage 5, challenge tiers afterward, and build-defining systems via Character, Sticker upgrades, Stamp loadout, and consumable Tiles. This strongly supports a strategic word-puzzle identity with roguelite/deck-growth structure rather than an action genre. Combat is metaphorical ('boss encounters') but the gameplay described is non-combat, methodical, and score/synergy focused.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Run-based, highly replayable synergy builder built around breaking a familiar ruleset for huge score combos, with escalating blinds/boss-like run structure.
Word construction is directly tied to combat, upgrades, and enemy encounters, making it one of the clearest word-game-meets-run-based-progression comparisons.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify the game as a minigolf roguelite: each run is different, levels have their own rules, players spend shots as score, collect coins for multipliers, buy equipment/power-ups, and progress through gates toward higher stages. This is strong evidence for a run-based score-chasing sports/arcade structure with roguelite modifiers. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a fitting minigolf/golf-arcade/roguelite sports archetype. 'sports_sim' is a poor fit because the game is not presented as simulation-focused, and other archetypes are even less appropriate. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest fit here: golf-centric run structure, every shot matters, hazard-heavy course progression, and modifier/power-up systems align well with ROGOLF's roguelite minigolf loop.
Very relevant as arcade minigolf with gimmick-heavy holes, challenges, powerups, and score chasing, but it lacks the run-based roguelite progression that defines ROGOLF.
Strong minigolf physics and obstacle-course identity make it understandable, but its multiplayer-party focus is a notable mismatch with ROGOLF's single-run progression and upgrade economy.
Run-based physics shots, shop/relic upgrades, score-efficient pathing, and roguelite progression map closely to the run structure.
Pinball-like projectile aiming with roguelite runs, shops, and relic builds; strong fit for the upgrade-driven bounce-shot loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Breath of Fire IV as a classic single-player JRPG adventure with a fixed cast of party members, skill and magic mastery, strategic battles, dragon transformations, and an authored story following Ryu, Nina, and companions across a fantasy world. Steam and Metacritic both support a long-form campaign structure and JRPG identity; the gameplay copy emphasizes party-based strategic combat and story progression, not match-based or fighting-game play. The current computed near-miss for traditional_fighter appears to come from misleading phrases like 'go head to head' and 'combo actions,' but the broader evidence clearly supports a story-driven party RPG.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Traditional single-player JRPG with a strong authored story, fixed party roles, town-and-dungeon progression, and menu-driven turn-based combat.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Chrono Cross: The Radical Dreamers Edition
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Story-focused late-PS1 JRPG with party management, distinctive character abilities, and progression built around strategic encounter planning.

current_live_neighbors.title: Monster Hunter Stories 2: Wings of Ruin

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly supports classic survival horror. Steam and corpus text explicitly state third person perspective, horror/zombie themes, escaping Raccoon City alive, and relying on brute force and cunning against terrifying mutations. Metacritic directly says it is part of the series that made the survival horror genre. The descriptions imply a linear authored campaign with encounter-driven enemy pressure, deliberate survival-oriented combat, and a single-player focus; Mercenaries Mode adds a secondary match-style mode without changing the core identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Same classic Resident Evil structure: fixed-camera exploration, inventory pressure, puzzles, zombies, and intertwined Raccoon City survival horror.
Shares the core mansion-era survival horror template of limited ammo, deliberate combat, key-item routing, and puzzle-driven progression.
Closely related classic survival horror focused on sparse resources, exploration, puzzles, and vulnerability, though with less action emphasis.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/meta descriptions consistently frame the game as a horror-focused survival experience: 'ultimate test of survival,' 'face your fears,' 'terror-filled classic,' with relentless zombies and mutant creatures. Metacritic explicitly tags the genre as Survival, and the copy describes two authored character campaigns centered on escaping a biotech outbreak in Raccoon City. The presentation is described as cutting-edge 3-D/photorealistic for its era, supporting third-person 3D survival-horror identity. Evidence is strong for survival_horror at the archetype level, though several lower-level mechanical facets like exact topology and combat structure are not explicitly described in the stored text.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Near-direct gameplay match: same classic Resident Evil survival-horror loop, fixed-camera era design, resource tension, puzzle-gated exploration, and closely shared audience expectations.

Still a Resident Evil survival-horror campaign with item management and escape pressure, but the remake's modern over-the-shoulder presentation, more action-forward pacing, and bundled multiplayer make it less identity-close to RE2 (1998).
Direct series template for fixed-camera survival horror, limited resources, mansion-style routing, and puzzle-gated progression.
Closely aligned in late-90s survival horror structure, with exploration, puzzles, constrained combat, and oppressive traversal through hostile spaces.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently describes the classic survival-horror loop: explore a remote mansion and connected areas, scavenge weapons from dead teammates, face man-eating creatures, and solve puzzles/disarm traps while uncovering the mystery. The copy strongly supports a single-player authored campaign in a horror setting with deliberate combat pressure and environmental puzzle gating. Mentions of ever-changing camera angles also support fixed-camera third-person presentation typical of survival horror.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The closest provided match: classic-era Resident Evil with fixed cameras, tank-style survival-horror pacing, puzzle-key progression, inventory pressure, and closely shared audience expectations.

Preserves the exploration-puzzle-survival loop of Resident Evil, but the over-the-shoulder camera, modern controls, and more kinetic presentation make it less like the 1996 original than the 1998 classic does.

staged_neighbors.requested_title: Alone in the Dark: The New Nightmare
staged_neighbors.why_similar: A legitimate survival-horror neighbor with mansion-style exploration, puzzle solving, combat scarcity, and dual protagonists, but it is a modern reimagining rather than a near-match to RE1's exact fixed-camera classic structure.
Classic-era Resident Evil with fixed cameras, tank controls, limited ammo, puzzle locks, and constant survival pressure.
Another foundational fixed-camera survival-horror game built around exploration, puzzle gating, authored progression, and vulnerability.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 2D side-scrolling action title centered on platforming and repeated boss battles. The strongest signals are side-scrolling presentation, dominant melee combat, boss-centric structure, and a single-player campaign loop. The copy emphasizes combos, unlockable skills and charms, and preparing builds before battles, which supports action-platformer progression with some metroidvania/beat-em-up adjacency. However, exploration, backtracking, and discovery are not described strongly enough to make metroidvania the primary archetype, and the boss-focused structure is less typical of a pure beat 'em up.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor with the same side-scrolling melee action, hand-drawn presentation, fourth-wall humor, and Morkull-led combat framework.
2D melee-focused exploration and boss fights with charm-based loadout customization and precision platform-combat pacing.
Side-scrolling action-platforming built around melee combos, traversal challenges, and a playful comedic tone.
Highly recognizable boss-centric 2D action game with repeated attempts, pattern learning, and hand-crafted animation, though more ranged and run-and-gun.
Boss-heavy 2D action-RPG/platformer with deliberate melee combat, build options, and a stronger challenge loop centered on difficult encounters.

current_live_neighbors.title: Chronicles of Teddy: Harmony of Exidus

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as visual_novel based on the strongest stored evidence: Metacritic explicitly labels the game as 'Visual Novel', and the only available description centers on a non-combat, narrative-driven cohabitation/relationship premise over a fixed three-year story arc. The text supports authored story progression, interpersonal focus, and a single long-form campaign. The computed JRPG/monster-collect signals appear over-inferred from sparse text and are contradicted by the direct genre label plus the absence of concrete evidence for combat, party control, or creature collection.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Classic raising sim built around scheduling, nurturing, and shaping a girl’s growth over several in-game years toward different outcomes.
Stat-building, time management, and event-driven choices determine character development and ending paths across a structured campaign timeline.
Character-management sim about caring for and influencing a partner-like figure through repeated choices, stat shifts, and branching outcomes.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies the game as a first-person narrative experience about reliving the life of Jesus, exploring Holy Land locations, meeting biblical figures, resisting temptation, reading scripture, praying, and performing miracles. However, the available copy does not establish a clear gameplay structure that maps strongly to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. There is no strong evidence for shooter, stealth, survival, RPG, immersive sim, puzzle, platforming, management, tactics, or sports/racing identity. Combat is unclear, progression is unclear, and the phrase 'simulation' appears only as a storefront genre label rather than a described systems loop. This looks closer to a first-person narrative/adventure or walking-sim style experience, which is not represented in the allowed archetype list, so classification would be too speculative.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on first-person authored narrative progression, scene-based interaction, and minimal traditional challenge; differs mainly in anthology structure and more varied vignette mechanics.
Strong fit as a first-person story-driven exploration game built around walking, dialogue, and guided plot beats with little combat.
Matches the slow-paced first-person exploration and environmental storytelling angle well, though it is more open and interpretive with less explicit directed interaction.
Shares the linear first-person narrative focus and low challenge model, but its no-goals meta-commentary format is more austere and less adventure-like than the anchor.
First-person narrative exploration built around scripted reenactment scenes and light interaction rather than combat or complex puzzles.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports an arcade-style undead survival action game with solo/local co-op, leaderboard competition, 20 stages, weapons/items, and Survival/Arcade modes. However, the evidence does not clearly establish a supported taxonomy archetype: perspective is unspecified, moment-to-moment structure is too vague to distinguish between beat_em_up, action_horror, co_op_horror, or another action subtype, and the current computed near-misses (party_game, sports_sim) are clearly false positives from sparse metadata. Because the core gameplay identity cannot be mapped confidently to one of the allowed archetypes, rejection is safer than forcing a weak fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent match on arcade wave survival, weapon pickups, local co-op, survival modes, and score/replay focus; closest overall gameplay identity despite broader perk progression.

Very strong arcade-action comp with short intense levels, leaderboard/scoring emphasis, local co-op, and old-school twin-stick sensibility; less survival-horror themed but highly aligned structurally.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Zombies Ate My Neighbors and Ghoul Patrol
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Classic 16-bit co-op monster-blasting action with stage progression, weapon variety, and horror-comedy arcade pacing; a strong stylistic and structural neighbor.
Strong zombie-focused arcade shooter with co-op and combat-heavy progression, but it is more modern and campaign-driven than the anchor’s compact retro leaderboard-style setup.
Small-scale stage-based arcade shooter with local co-op, score attack structure, and intense replayable combat despite the non-undead theme.

removed_candidates: THE HOUSE OF THE DEAD: Remake

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly and explicitly call Fishbowl a 'visual novel' and a narrative slice-of-life coming-of-age story. The core loop centers on making choices over the course of a month, video-calling friends/family/coworkers, doing daily care/work tasks, and sorting through belongings and memory-related puzzles. The text stresses 'no bad endings, just different paths' and says 'the choices you make will shape the possibilities in Alo's future,' which strongly supports authored branching and dialogue/relationship-focused interaction. No combat is described anywhere; instead the gameplay is framed as intimate, cozy, grief-focused narrative progression with light puzzle/matching activities. That combination fits visual_novel much better than broader adventure labels.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Interactive visual novel structure, intimate coming-of-age storytelling, month-spanning personal narrative, and low-challenge play make this the closest match in gameplay identity and audience expectation.

Very strong narrative-first, cozy, conversation-led fit with light routine mechanics and branching outcomes, though the drink-mixing service loop is more central than Fishbowl's memory/daily-life structure.
Shares gentle narrative pacing, community interactions, family history, and surreal/emotional elements, but it is more exploratory adventure-driven and less visual-novel-like in core play.

Strong thematic overlap in memory-focused, grief-adjacent, symbolic storytelling with very light interaction, but it is more of a short linear interactive art piece than a choice-shaped daily-life narrative.
Conversation-led narrative game structured around repeated daily shifts, relationship choices, and emotional decision-making with light systems.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a relaxing environmental restoration/cleanup game focused on vacuuming waste, recycling materials, rebuilding damaged structures, exploring large maps, and cooperating with up to 25 players. The strongest supported traits are multiplayer/co-op support, large shared spaces, casual/simulation framing, and non-combat restorative play. However, the Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a suitable home for a cooperative cleanup/restoration exploration sim with minimal or no combat. The closest available archetypes are poor fits because they require identity-defining elements not supported here, such as farming/life loops, factory/engineering systems, colony/city management, survival pressures, shooter combat, or RPG progression. Because the gameplay identity is clear but falls outside the available archetype set, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate category.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on relaxing environmental cleanup, vacuum/tool use, visible restoration, light exploration, and upgrade-based efficiency; mainly differs by being single-player.
Excellent fit on satisfying tool-driven cleanup, low-pressure pacing, and progression through restored spaces, though it is more job-based washing than ecosystem restoration/exploration.
Strong restoration-first match with pollution cleanup, recycling, upgrades, exploration, and reviving ecosystems; creature-care elements make it a bit more bespoke than the anchor.

Shares vacuuming trash, recycling, light exploration, and environmental restoration, but farming, village rebuilding, and life-sim structure pull it away from the anchor’s cleaner pure restoration loop.
Restore dirty, damaged spaces by cleaning debris, repairing structures, and visibly improving environments in a relaxed sim loop.
